channel.title: Daily Briefing
channel.description: A daily briefing on weather, tech, health, business, theology, philosophy, and clear thinking.

item.title: Daily Briefing — 2026-03-20
item.description:
Weather

The atmospheric river that has been hammering the Fraser Valley all week is finally tapering, but not gently. Environment Canada has upgraded the rainfall warning to orange — "significant damage likely" — with another 10–20 mm expected before showers ease near noon. High of 11°C with southwest winds gusting to 50 km/h through the afternoon, overnight low of 1°C — frost risk tonight. The FVRD declared a state of emergency yesterday; roughly 40 homes along the Chilliwack River remain under evacuation alert, though sandbagging efforts are paying off. A mudslide in Coquitlam forced an airlift of one couple yesterday. Saturday: 9°C, scattered showers. Sunday drier at 11°C. Trails saturated through the weekend.

Tech

Claude Code

Two releases in two days. Claude Code v2.1.81 shipped today with a --bare flag for scripted -p calls — skips hooks, LSP, plugin sync, and skill directory walks. Also: --channels permission relay, OAuth token refresh fixes, and MCP tool call output that collapses into a single line. Yesterday's v2.1.80 added rate_limits for statusline scripts, inline plugin declarations via source: 'settings', effort frontmatter for skills, and fixed --resume dropping parallel tool results.

Claude Platform

Anthropic published a survey of ~81,000 people across 159 countries on AI attitudes — Sub-Saharan Africa and Asia substantially more optimistic than the West. The DOJ urged a court to reject Anthropic's preliminary injunction against the Pentagon's supply-chain risk designation. Hearing on temporary relief set for March 24.

Competitors

OpenAI rolled out GPT-5.4 Mini to ChatGPT Free/Go users. OpenAI also confirmed ending Instant Checkout, pivoting to retailer apps. Gemini got a Workspace update — contextual drafting in Docs, AI spreadsheets in Sheets, deck generation in Slides, AI Overviews in Drive.

Apple

Tim Cook: the Mac just had its "best launch week ever for first-time Mac customers". MacBook Neo demand outstripping supply — orders today show April 6–13 delivery. New iPhone Fold patents and Apple Watch design filings.

General Interest

Hacker News: "AI coding is gambling" tops (420 pts, 343 comments). Also: "Show HN: Will my flight have Starlink?" (350 pts), CVE-2026-3888 Snap privilege escalation (148 comments), Google's "Sashiko" for agentic AI kernel code review.

Business

Markets

Third consecutive daily decline, fourth straight week in the red. S&P 500 fell 1.5% to 6,507, Nasdaq dropped 2.0% to 21,654, Dow shed 0.96% to 45,577. Russell 2000 in correction territory. Brent topped $120/bbl, gold hit $4,667. Planet Labs surged 26% on breakeven Q4.

Canada Macro

Bank of Canada held at 2.25% Wednesday. CPI eased to 1.8% but BoC warned Iran war will boost energy prices. Unemployment at 6.7%. Next: April 29.

US Macro

Fed held at 3.50–3.75%, 11-1 vote. PCE inflation raised to 2.7%. 92,000 jobs cut in February, unemployment 4.4%. Seven of 19 FOMC officials now expect no cuts this year.

Psalm of the Day — Psalm 1

The Two Ways

1Blessed is the man who walks not in the counsel of the wicked, nor stands in the way of sinners, nor sits in the seat of scoffers; 2but his delight is in the law of the LORD, and on his law he meditates day and night. 3He is like a tree planted by streams of water that yields its fruit in its season, and its leaf does not wither. In all that he does, he prospers. 4The wicked are not so, but are like chaff that the wind drives away. 5Therefore the wicked will not stand in the judgment, nor sinners in the congregation of the righteous; 6for the LORD knows the way of the righteous, but the way of the wicked will perish.

Scripture quotations are from the ESV® Bible, © 2001 by Crossway. Used by permission. All rights reserved.

Exegesis

Psalm 1 is a Torah psalm — a wisdom composition placed at the gate of the Psalter to frame everything that follows as meditation on divine instruction. The architecture is binary: two ways, two images. The righteous are a tree (ʿēṣ), planted and fruitful; the wicked are chaff (môṣ), weightless and wind-scattered. The opening ʾašrê ("blessed") is a declaration, not a prayer. The three verbs — walks, stands, sits — trace descent from casual association to settled identification with wickedness. The Hebrew tôrâ carries the broader sense of "instruction." The tree image draws on ANE royal iconography but democratizes it. The closing yāda' ("knows") operates in its covenantal register: intimate, committed relationship. The psalm offers no third option.

SEP — Formal Epistemology

plato.stanford.edu/entries/formal-epistemology/

Formal epistemology applies mathematical tools — probability theory, decision theory, modal logic — to traditional epistemological questions. Bayesian epistemology models belief as degrees of credence and uses conditionalization on evidence to update them. Accuracy-first epistemology (Joyce, Pettigrew) derives probabilism from the norm that credences should minimize distance from truth. The field also covers belief revision (AGM framework), epistemic logic, and epistemic game theory. Critics argue formalization purchases precision at the cost of philosophical depth; defenders reply that informal epistemology has spent centuries circling the same problems. As the entry notes, formal epistemology is "an expression of the hope that reason can be productively turned upon itself."

Cognitive Bias — Anchoring

Anchoring is the tendency to rely too heavily on the first piece of information you encounter. Tversky and Kahneman demonstrated this with a wheel of fortune — those who saw 65 estimated higher than those who saw 10, even though the number was random. The same mechanism operates in salary negotiations, real estate, and courtroom sentencing. Knowing about anchoring does not make you immune. The corrective: generate your own independent estimate before responding to any initial figure.
